items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон.
items.translation:
I’m plagued by vague misgivings again...
Shpak lost a tape recorder,
the ambassador lost a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Mostly successful: it preserves the mock-heroic phrasing (“plagued by vague misgivings”) that carries the joke. Crucially, it also avoids the wrong possessive reading (“Shpak has...”), by making the loss explicit (“lost”), which matches the reference note that these items are missing. Minor stylistic nuance: “haunt me” is a bit more elevated/theatrical than “I’m plagued by,” but the intended high-brow self-dramatization is still there.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—very pleased.
Tsar—very pleased, Tsar.
Very pleased—hello, Tsar.

Tsar—very pleased.
Hello, Tsar—very pleased!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: Works well: it keeps the repetitive, awkwardly polite Soviet-style greeting ritual, which is the core comedic effect (banal etiquette colliding with “tsar” roleplay and drunkenness). It’s not identical to the reference, but it achieves the same comic mechanism. Slight fluency/formatting awkwardness from repeated line breaks and em-dash fragments (“Tsar—very pleased”) that can read a bit choppy in English, though choppiness arguably mirrors the drunken repetition.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Essentially perfect: preserves the authoritarian, performative demand and the sudden power-trip tone. The missing “that” is a negligible stylistic difference and does not affect the punch.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали.
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the reference; perfect. It keeps the elevated/archaic melancholy register that makes the line funny in a modern domestic setting.

items.translation:
They walled me up...
They walled me up, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Very close and functionally equivalent: the key is the paranoid-mystical misreading of a mundane mechanical situation, with “demons” as the agent. “Walled me up” is slightly less idiomatic than “walled me in,” but still clear and keeps the comic accusation.

items.translation: See what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Good: preserves the religious triumphalism and causal attribution to the cross (the joke in an atheist/modern-tech context). Slightly less grand than “Behold the power of…”, but still conveys the same performative certainty and register shift.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Meaning is conveyed (a command to start dancing), but the creative property in the reference is the quasi-announcer/MC formula (“The floor is open…”) that makes it feel like a hosted social event and heightens the anachronistic Soviet-party vibe. “Everybody dance!” is flatter and more generic, losing some of the specific comic framing and performative ‘opening the dance floor’ feel.

items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Acceptable but slightly off in character flavor. The line is a breezy, self-satisfied thief’s catchphrase; “What a lucky break!” captures the luck but loses the colloquial ‘I dropped in at just the right time’ smugness and the sense of casual criminal opportunism. Still understandable and mildly comic, but less quotable/idiomatic to the situation than the reference’s ‘good call’/‘good time to stop by’ feel.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference; perfect. It preserves the Soviet-food-shortage satire and correctly avoids calling the eggplant spread “caviar,” which would confuse and blunt the joke.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три.
items.translation:
Everything I earned
through backbreaking labor—

all gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket... three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Largely successful: it keeps the escalating inventory rhythm and the comedic trailing self-correction (“a suede jacket... three”). “Domestic cigarette cases” is a good rendering of «отечественных». The added framing “Everything I earned through backbreaking labor—all gone” is present in the surrounding dialogue in the source and is fine in context, but for this specific meme-line the reference emphasizes the list itself; still, the list’s comic counting is preserved. Minor fluency: “a suede jacket... three” is intentionally odd, but it lands as the intended bungled accounting.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!
And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Good: preserves the calm, rhythmic, resigned refrain that turns the psychiatric threat into a shared absurdist punchline. Exclamation marks slightly increase emotional intensity compared to the more deadpan ellipses, but the comedic effect remains intact.

summary.interpretation: 1.09 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.22 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
